food_subgroup: Crustaceans — whole crab, steamed or boiled

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Dog Mother Crab (Thai whole crab dish). FOODON_03302757 'crab food product' is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ 'Crabs' is the closest parent category. No specific code exists for the Thai Dog Mother Crab preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226793008 'Crab (food)' is the closest available concept; no specific code for this Thai preparation exists.

main_ingredients: whole mud crab or blue swimmer crab

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Dog Mother Crab is one whole crab weighing approximately 400 g as served, which includes the shell; edible meat yield is approximately 30–35% of total weight.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, steamed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15139)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Dog Mother Crab is a Thai whole-crab dish (typically mud crab or blue swimmer crab) prepared by steaming or boiling with Thai aromatics. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for steamed crab meat (blue crab, cooked, moist heat, NDB 15139). The Thai aromatics and dipping sauce contribute negligible macronutrient change to the crab meat itself per 100 g edible portion. Values represent the edible crab meat portion per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Research Group
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dog Mother Crab. Steamed crab meat contains negligible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g edible portion); therefore the glycemic index is effectively zero. By convention for very-low-carbohydrate protein foods (analogous to steamed fish and shellfish, GI ~5 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables), GI is set to 5 (low). Glycemic load per 400 g serving is 0.0 due to zero carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from crab meat and fish sauce dipping accompaniment
hypertension.guidance: Steamed crab meat contains approximately 395 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as fish sauce or soy sauce typically served alongside this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and high-quality lean protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dog Mother Crab is an excellent protein source with virtually zero carbohydrate, resulting in a glycemic load of zero per serving, making it a suitable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content and sodium level balanced against omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crab meat provides beneficial omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and lean protein but contains approximately 100 mg cholesterol per 100 g and elevated sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume in moderation and minimise accompanying high-sodium condiments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dog Mother Crab is a Thai seafood dish featuring a whole crab — typically a mud crab (Scylla serrata) or blue swimmer crab — prepared by steaming or boiling with Thai aromatic herbs including lemongrass, galangal, and kaffir lime leaves, then served with a spicy Thai dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, chilli, and garlic. The dish is popular in Thai seafood restaurants and has gained a following in Singapore's Thai restaurant scene, where it is often priced as a premium whole-crab offering. The name 'Dog Mother Crab' is a colloquial English rendering of the Thai concept of a large, egg-bearing female crab (แม่ปู, mae puu), prized for its roe and rich flavour. The flesh is sweet, firm, and slightly briny, with a delicate umami depth enhanced by the aromatic steaming liquid. Nutritionally, crab meat is an excellent source of high-quality lean protein (approximately 19 g per 100 g edible portion), very low in fat and carbohydrate, and rich in vitamin B12, selenium, zinc, and phosphorus. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, it is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic load, though the sodium content from both the crab itself and accompanying fish sauce dipping condiments warrants attention for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is typically served as a shared centrepiece at a Thai seafood meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Dog Mother Crab?
Is Dog Mother Crab high in sodium?
What type of crab is used in Dog Mother Crab?
How is Dog Mother Crab prepared?
What does Dog Mother Crab taste like?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Dog Mother Crab exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy steamed blue crab data (NDB 15139), which is the closest defensible approved-source match for steamed whole crab edible meat. GI is estimated by analogy from published international GI tables for very-low-carbohydrate shellfish. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for steamed crab meat — crab meat contains no glycemic carbohydrates by nature (naturally zero). Sugar_g is 0.0: naturally zero as crab meat contains no free sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as this is an animal-derived seafood product with no plant fibre. Trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for steamed crab. 2. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used for this record. 3. Glycemic index set to 5 (effectively zero) as this dish contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per convention for very-low-carbohydrate protein foods, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database analogous shellfish entries and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. 4. Nutrient values represent the edible crab meat portion per 100 g and do not include the shell weight. The Thai aromatic steaming herbs and dipping sauce contribute negligible macronutrient change to the crab meat per 100 g edible portion. 5. The dish name 'Dog Mother Crab' is a colloquial English rendering used by the restaurant Shrimp Prawn Seafood - Boat Quay on field survey data; the Thai culinary concept refers to a large female crab (แม่ปู). 6. Halal status is assessed as likely halal (crab is permissible under most Shafi'i interpretations followed in Singapore/Thailand), but formal halal certification of the specific restaurant has not been verified. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are sourced from USDA SR Legacy NDB 15139 (blue crab, cooked, moist heat).
